title: Routing washing machine greywater to the garden
subtitle: Diverting laundry wastewater to irrigate non-edible plants safely and legally in the UK
excerpt: Washing machine wastewater (greywater) can be diverted to irrigate lawns, ornamentals, and trees rather than draining directly to sewer. A household washing machine uses 40-75 litres per cycle; a family running 5 cycles per week produces 200-375 litres that would otherwise go to waste. This covers the diverter valve approach, detergent selection, and UK legal requirements.
sourceNotes: Environment Agency (EA) greywater reuse guidance. CIWEM (Chartered Institution of Water and Environmental Management) greywater reuse guidance. Water UK member company byelaw information. Garden Organic advice on safe greywater garden use. WRAP water efficiency guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wastewater from washing machines, baths, showers, and hand basins. Greywater contains soaps and detergents but not faecal matter (which is blackwater). In the UK, greywater reuse is permitted for garden irrigation and toilet flushing under specific conditions.

content.text: from a washing machine contains detergent residues, fibres, and trace contaminants from clothing. It is not clean water. For non-edible plants it is an acceptable irrigation source. It also saves a meaningful amount of water during summer dry spells.

Diverting washing machine water to a garden is legal in England, Wales, and Scotland for watering non-edible plants below the surface. You must not create puddles or runoff onto a road or a neighbour's property. Some water companies have old rules restricting this; check with your supplier first. No permit is needed from the Environment Agency.

Do not apply laundry greywater to: fruit and vegetables where water contacts the edible portion, root vegetables, salad leaves, or children's play areas. The detergent content and residual microbial load make this a food safety risk. Lawns, ornamental beds, shrubs, and trees are the appropriate targets.

Most laundry detergents contain optical brighteners and sometimes biocides. These build up in soil and harm its microbial life. Switch to a plant-based detergent with no optical brighteners or fabric conditioner. Soap nuts or castile soap liquid both work. Avoid antibacterial detergents or bleach if the water will go to the garden.
Locate the washing machine drain hose. In most UK installations it connects to a standpipe or trap under the sink. The hose is typically 22 mm diameter.

Fit a three-way diverter valve on the drain hose. Diverter valves with a manual or automatic lever let you switch between sewer and garden routes. Automatic models switch based on water pressure during the drain pump cycle.

Route the garden hose to a collection butt or directly to a soaker hose laid on an ornamental bed. A 200-litre collection butt gives flexibility to apply water when needed rather than forcing it to the garden in the middle of heavy rain.

Return the valve to sewer position during winter or when using any bleach or fabric conditioner cycle. Greywater stored longer than 24 hours becomes anaerobic and odorous; do not store it for extended periods.

Sodium-based detergents can raise the sodium level in soil over several seasons and weaken its structure. To prevent this, alternate greywater irrigation with fresh rainwater to flush sodium through. Add gypsum (calcium sulphate) once a year to beds that receive the most greywater. A light annual compost mulch also keeps the microbial balance healthy.

attrs.symptom: Collection butt is filling faster than the garden needs water
attrs.cause: In wet weather or winter, outflow exceeds demand
attrs.fix: Switch the diverter back to sewer during periods of rain or low garden demand. Greywater should not overflow the butt onto impermeable surfaces where it might run off to drainage.

attrs.symptom: Plants on the receiving bed are yellowing or growing poorly
attrs.cause: Detergent accumulation or sodium build-up is affecting soil pH and structure
attrs.fix: Stop applying greywater to that bed for the rest of the season. Apply gypsum at 100 g per m2 and water in. Switch to a different plant-safe detergent before resuming the following year.
